main_summary.summary: Norma Mtume, born Norma Stoker in San Diego in 1949 and raised in South Central Los Angeles, was interviewed by David Cline on 27 June 2016 in Los Angeles for the Civil Rights History Project. Mtume describes graduating from Jefferson High School in 1967, attending Cal State LA on a $250 scholarship, meeting her first husband Albert Armour through the Black Student Union, and joining the Los Angeles chapter of the Black Panther Party in January 1970 after the December 1969 police raids on BPP offices. At nineteen she coordinated the Alprentice "Bunchy" Carter People's Free Clinic at 32nd and Central, where Marie F. Branch and Dr. Terry Kupers mentored a volunteer staff of physicians, nursing students, and pharmaceutical reps. After relocating to the Bay Area in 1972, Mtume directed the George Jackson Free Clinic in Berkeley, then was appointed Minister of Finance under Elaine Brown in late 1974. She recounts her 1977 arrest tied to Huey P. Newton's witness-targeting incident, her two-year California State Prison sentence served at Frontera, and her subsequent career running SHIELDS for Families, the Compton-based substance-abuse and family-services nonprofit she helped grow from $500,000 to $25 million over 24 years. She closes describing her current community-faculty work at Charles R. Drew University of Medicine and Science.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Mtume offers one of the few sustained first-person accounts of how the Black Panther Party's free-clinic infrastructure was actually built and operated by a coordinator who started at age nineteen with no formal medical training. Her testimony documents the clinical staffing model (Marie F. Branch as RN-mentor, pharmaceutical-rep sample donations, volunteer pre-med students from UC Berkeley's Black Health Science Caucus, Vietnam corpsmen contributing emergency-care skills) and the specific public-health interventions the Party undertook, including STI screening protocols within the membership and shigella-outbreak containment.

The interview also documents her presence at the financial and organizational center of the BPP during the Elaine Brown leadership transition (1974-1977), including the school's 501(c)(3) incorporation, law-enforcement grant funding, and the internal tithing system. Her account of the events leading to her expulsion and prosecution provides primary-source material on the late-period internal crisis under Huey P. Newton's return from Cuba. Her subsequent SHIELDS for Families work, addressing prenatal drug exposure at Martin Luther King Jr.-Drew Medical Center, directly extends the Party's health-disparities work into the post-movement nonprofit sector.

chapters.title: The Sun Returns
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Los Angeles in June 2016, Norma Mtume gives her birth name Norma Stoker, her married names Armour and Mtume, and explains that her husband Omawale Mtume took a Kiswahili name meaning the sun returns, shedding the slave names many were discarding in the 1960s.

chapters.title: Twenty-First and Naomi
chapters.summary: Mtume describes her parents, a Louisiana-raised mother and a Texas-born father who became a librarian, the family's move from San Diego to Los Angeles, and growing up poor in South Central around 21st and Naomi, where children wore cardboard in their shoes and no one felt poor because everyone had the same.

chapters.title: A Scholarship of Two Hundred Fifty Dollars
chapters.summary: She recalls boyfriends and neighbors being drafted to Vietnam and not coming back the same, graduating from the nearly all-Black Jefferson High School in 1967 just after the Watts uprising, and going on to Cal State LA on a $250 scholarship to study math and physical education.

chapters.title: The Blue VW and the December Raid
chapters.summary: Mtume tells how she met her first husband Albert Armour, a Black Student Union activist with a blue VW, Panther stickers, and a Muhammad Speaks newspaper that upset her mother, and how after the December 1969 police raid hospitalized him with a broken arm and damaged eye socket she began volunteering at the 44th Street office, typing memos and working the breakfast program.

chapters.title: Nineteen and Running the Clinic
chapters.summary: At nineteen Mtume became coordinator of the clinic with help from volunteer professionals, the RN and UCLA instructor Marie Branch and the psychiatrist Dr. Terry Kupers, who recruited residents, doctors, and nursing students; she connects this to her present work on health disparities at Charles R. Drew University.

chapters.title: A Storefront and Point Number Four
chapters.summary: Mtume describes the self-study that prepared her and the storefront clinic at 32nd and Central, built out into three exam rooms, a pharmacy, and a lab and stocked with free drug samples the doctors got from pharmaceutical reps, all answering point number four of the party's ten-point platform for decent health care.

chapters.title: Up at Five-Thirty for the Breakfast Program
chapters.summary: Mtume recounts giving up her apartment to live in the party facilities with her son, the nursery that cared for members' children, and the punishing days that began before dawn with the breakfast program and ran past ten with evening clinics, political education classes, and the guard shifts everyone took so that what happened to Fred Hampton would not happen to them.

chapters.title: That's My Brother
chapters.summary: Mtume tells of being stopped while leaving to do communal laundry, an officer producing a portfolio of photos of party members while she called her husband's picture her brother, the constant surveillance that fell hardest on the men, and the high stress that drove some members to alcohol and drugs despite the party's rule that they clean up.

chapters.title: Served the People Body and Soul
chapters.summary: Mtume reflects on the party's principle of equality between men and women against the chauvinism members carried in from a patriarchal society, the growing number of women in leadership, and the deep lifelong bond among veteran Panther women who trusted each other with their lives.

chapters.title: A Bus to San Quentin
chapters.topic: Family and the Free Busing Program
chapters.summary: Mtume describes the party's free busing-to-prisons program that finally let her mother visit her oldest brother at San Quentin, easing her mother's fears, and her father's worry for her safety, then turns to her earliest memory of discrimination on childhood drives to Texas around 1955 and 1956.

chapters.title: He Went Around to the Back Door
chapters.summary: She recalls a Texas trip at about age nine when, with the Black motels in El Paso full during a Baptist convention, her father slept by the roadside under willow trees and later entered a whites-only restaurant by the back door for coffee and food, then showed her the separate Black pool, school, and the bleachers far behind the drive-in screen.

chapters.title: Pre-Food-Bank Survival
chapters.summary: Mtume catalogs the survival programs: free food before food banks existed, free coats for cold cities, the SAFE program escorting seniors, outreach clinics modeled on the barefoot doctors of Cuba and China, legal aid and paralegals, and the Oakland nursery and school that cared for children around the clock so parents could work long hours.

chapters.part: Bunchy Carter and the US Conflict
chapters.title: The Bunchy Carter People's Free Clinic
chapters.topic: Naming the Clinic and the US Rivalry
chapters.summary: Mtume explains the clinic was named the Alprentice Bunchy Carter People's Free Clinic for the man who, with John Huggins, founded the Los Angeles chapter, recalls meeting Carter once at a rally, and recounts the rivalry with Ron Karenga's US Organization, learned firsthand from her husband at UCLA when Carter and Huggins were killed, contrasting the Panthers' Russian, Chinese, and Cuban revolutionary ideology with the US Organization's Afrocentric focus.

chapters.part: Bunchy Carter and the US Conflict
chapters.title: A Girl With Six Brothers
chapters.summary: Mtume tells of being attacked by US Organization members while selling papers pregnant and fending them off, her lasting refusal to be in Karenga's presence over later acts she attributes to him, and her belief that Karenga gave the order, possibly with police collaboration, to shoot Carter and Huggins.

chapters.part: Bunchy Carter and the US Conflict
chapters.title: Corners and Ideologies
chapters.summary: She places the Carter and Huggins killing in early 1969 before she joined, describes the street corners staked out by the Black Panthers, the US Organization, the Nation of Islam, and the Holy Ghost Christians, and the Los Angeles gangs like the Slausons and Pueblos whose members brought misplaced aggression the party rechanneled once they gained consciousness.

chapters.title: The George Jackson Free Clinic
chapters.summary: Mtume describes being assigned during the move north to direct the George Jackson Free Clinic in Berkeley, collaborating with the Berkeley Free Clinic, the Women's Health Collective, and the Haight-Ashbury Free Clinic, and how UC Berkeley's Black Health Science Caucus and Vietnam corpsmen taught the workers to draw blood, do stitches on pigs' feet, and perform exams they practiced on each other, a practice that caught her own cervical cancer early.

chapters.title: Read It Like a Novel
chapters.summary: Mtume describes devouring the Merck Manual and the women's health collective book, teaching preventive self-care, running a communicable disease control program against shigella and chickenpox outbreaks, and in the free-love era screening every newcomer to town and publishing a list of those barred from sex until treated.

chapters.title: Minister of Finance
chapters.topic: Headquarters Books and a Strained Marriage
chapters.summary: Mtume describes her Bay Area years from 1972 to 1977, running the clinic until 1974, and how after Huey Newton and Bobby Seale left and Elaine Brown took charge, Brown asked her to become Minister of Finance, collecting the ten-percent tithe everyone with income paid and learning real bookkeeping once the school won grants, while the strain of Oakland kept her apart from her Los Angeles husband, who after developing multiple sclerosis became her finance assistant.

chapters.title: When Huey Came Back From Cuba
chapters.summary: She describes how the party shifted under Elaine Brown toward mainstream funding, including a law-enforcement grant and a social-service nonprofit wing housing the clinic, seniors, food, and clothing programs, and how Huey Newton's return from Cuba changed everything as his forward-thinking ideas strained the people around him.

chapters.title: The Wrong House
chapters.summary: Mtume relates that Newton, charged with shooting a prostitute, sent someone to kill the witness whose address had been published, that the team went to the wrong house where the woman shot through the door killing one of them, and that because members lived communally the dead man's license led police to search her home and haul out hidden weapons cases.

chapters.title: Knowledge, Possession, and Control
chapters.summary: She describes being arrested as members kept leaving, being kicked out of the party so its resources could defend Newton's case, fighting her own case for a year, and a two-year California state prison sentence built on the doctrine of knowledge, possession, and control because the house and utilities were in her name.

chapters.title: Do Not Pass Go
chapters.summary: Mtume recounts three months in the Frontera reception center while officials watched for her to start a riot, a single cell, becoming secretary of the Women's Advisory Council, and earning an AA inside, then after release earning a bachelor's and master's in health administration and at fifty-five a master's in marriage and family therapy.

chapters.title: A Condemned Trailer
chapters.summary: Mtume describes leaving paper-pushing county work to write grants for substance-abuse programs serving women whose children were born drug-exposed, a Diane Watson bill that followed over twelve hundred such births a year at Martin Luther King Jr. Drew Medical Center, and how she and her boss left the county to found their own nonprofit, SHIELDS, in a dilapidated trailer on Charles R. Drew land with a half-million-dollar grant and fourteen employees, soon buying apartments to house the near-homeless.

chapters.title: They Mirror the Party's Work
chapters.summary: Mtume describes adding mental health, vocational, and AIDS services as she became health officer and chief operating officer, growing SHIELDS over twenty-four years to three hundred fifty employees and twenty-five million dollars before she retired, and noting how its programs mirror the work she did in the party across Compton, Watts, and Willowbrook.

chapters.title: Two Steps Forward, Ten Back
chapters.summary: She voices concern that the Affordable Care Act is pushing substance-abuse treatment back into a medical model and excluding the recovering people who did the work, the discouragement of watching hard-won gains cycle backward, and her move into community faculty at Charles R. Drew University after retiring from SHIELDS.

chapters.title: Social Determinants of Health
chapters.summary: Mtume explains the community-faculty mission of teaching medical, nursing, and public-health students the social determinants of health, why a patient may not fill or trust a prescription, the resistance of older professors set in their ways, and her diversity-curriculum work with UCLA doctors and nurses now that she is semi-retired.

chapters.title: Survival Pending Revolution
chapters.summary: Asked how the Panthers are remembered, Mtume insists that self-defense mattered because the dead cannot fight, but that guns were never the point: the party ran a plethora of survival programs under the banner survival pending revolution to give all poor and oppressed people the best life possible. She speaks of her educator daughter who keeps lifelong party friends, her wish that young people step around the ditch the Panthers fell into, and coalitions with the Brown Berets and with Cesar Chavez and the farm workers.

chapters.title: A Day-to-Day Grinder
chapters.summary: Mtume describes coalitions with the Gray Panthers, students, and the anti-war and women's movements, joining the executive committee only as finance minister, and reflects that she never saw herself as a visionary but as a day-to-day grinder who, even through prison, kept moving up and never expected to reach where she is.

loc_video.caption: Norma Mtume oral history interview conducted bv David P. Cline in Los Angeles, California, 2016 June 27
